The open election data Malaysia deserves
ElectionData.MY is the public face of the Malaysian Election Corpus (MECo): a civic and academic effort to make Malaysian election data open, accessible, and usable.

ElectionData.MY has no political affiliation, institutional funding, or financial patronage influencing how data is curated or presented. It does not accept donations, provide paid consultation, or engage in any conduct (including advertising) that compromises its integrity as a compiler of high-quality election data.

Everything on the site is dedicated to the public domain (CC0): free forever, for any use, at the highest possible granularity. But openness alone is not enough—for many, technical complexity is just another kind of closed. ElectionData.MY strives to make data accessible to everyone: researchers, journalists, developers, and most importantly, voters.

Building this site has required manually digitising decades-old results, tracing historical boundary maps by hand, and spending countless hours on painstaking validation. ElectionData.MY operates with the view that obsessing over quality and completeness is worth it—so Malaysia has a source which people (and AI) can rely on.

Information on elections should be easy to find, and easy to use. In 2026, Malaysians should not have to rely on PDFs and scattered sources to deeply understand their own democracy. ElectionData.MY exists to fill that gap. It is the public face of the [Malaysian Election Corpus (MECo)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41597-025-06502-7): a civic and academic effort to digitise, clean, and open-source data on every Malaysian election from 1955 to the present day. Every candidate, every party, every result—in a single site built to make Malaysian election data genuinely accessible. ## A standing offer to SPR ElectionData.MY demonstrates that election data can be made open in a seamless and cost-efficient way. In an ideal world, SPR itself would already be performing this function, particularly given its access to the full ecosystem of election data. However, institutional change takes time. In the spirit of nation-building, I therefore make an unconditional offer to SPR: either to help SPR build and maintain an official open data platform from the ground up, or to partner directly with SPR so that ElectionData.MY becomes its official open data provider. Either way, the offer is entirely pro bono. No strings attached, and no expiry. The long-term hope is simple: that this platform becomes unnecessary—or at most, complementary to an official site. Until then, the work continues. _Thevesh Theva_
_Curator_
_ElectionData.MY_
Kami membinanya kerana ia patut wujud
ElectionData.MY ialah wajah awam Malaysian Election Corpus, yang menjadikan data pilihan raya Malaysia terbuka, boleh dicapai, dan boleh digunakan.

Setiap rekod dalam MECo telah dikurasi secara manual selama empat tahun: calon dipadankan merentasi pilihan raya, kesinambungan parti dijejaki, dan kesilapan dalam dokumen sumber dikenal pasti serta dibetulkan. Data ini disahkan berbanding keputusan rasmi baris demi baris. Ia bukan sekadar dikikis lalu diterbitkan; ia dibina.

Keterbukaan bermaksud CC0: tiada lesen untuk dinavigasi, tiada atribusi diperlukan, dan bebas untuk sebarang kegunaan. Kebolehcapaian pula bermaksud sesuatu yang lebih sukar: data itu benar-benar boleh digunakan oleh wartawan, penyelidik, pembangun, dan sesiapa sahaja yang memerlukannya.

ElectionData.MY tidak mempunyai gabungan politik, pembiayaan institusi, atau agenda. Data dipersembahkan seadanya, dengan tafsiran yang minimum. Apa maksud angka-angka itu diserahkan sepenuhnya kepada anda.

<ArticleBody>

ElectionData.MY bermula seperti kebanyakan projek sebegini: bukan dengan rancangan besar, tetapi dengan rasa tidak puas hati yang tidak dapat diselesaikan dengan cara lain. Pada tahun 2022, saya secara santai mula [mentweet data tentang pilihan raya yang bakal berlangsung](https://x.com/Thevesh/status/1585248940425105409), dan entah bagaimana ia mendapat perhatian. Tiba-tiba, saya menjadi "election data guy".

Kisah asalnya tidaklah begitu mulia. Dorongan yang suatu ketika dahulu membuat penciptanya menyelongkar gerai pasar malam untuk mencari kad Digimon akhirnya menemui saluran yang lebih bersifat sivik: data pilihan raya Malaysia.

Data itu wujud secara berpecah-pecah: tertanam dalam PDF, bertaburan dalam fail Excel yang diedarkan parti, tidak seragam formatnya, dan sukar dibandingkan merentasi masa. Tiada satu tempat pun yang menghimpunkan semuanya. Maka, satu tempat dibina.

Apa yang bermula sebagai koleksi peribadi menjadi Malaysian Election Corpus, atau MECo. ElectionData.MY ialah wajah awamnya: sebuah platform untuk mengakses, menanyakan, dan membina berdasarkan data menyeluruh tentang setiap pilihan raya yang diadakan di Malaysia dari tahun 1954 hingga kini.

Suruhanjaya Pilihan Raya Malaysia, SPR, tidak menerbitkan datanya dalam format yang mudah digunakan. ElectionData.MY wujud untuk mengisi jurang itu: bukan untuk menggantikan SPR, tetapi untuk melakukan apa yang patut dilakukan oleh platform data terbuka. Menjadikan rekod awam boleh dicapai.

Skopnya merangkumi setiap calon dan parti yang pernah bertanding dalam pilihan raya Malaysia. Keputusan peringkat Parlimen dan DUN, sempadan geospatial, daftar pemilih yang dipseudonimkan, pecahan peringkat saluran. Semuanya percuma, semuanya dalam domain awam.

</ArticleBody>

<CorePrinciples title="Prinsip teras kami" />

<ArticleBody>

ElectionData.MY ialah pertaruhan jangka panjang seorang individu bahawa apabila data tersedia, orang ramai boleh melakukan lebih banyak perkara dengannya. Wartawan menemui cerita dengan lebih pantas. Penyelidik bertanya soalan yang lebih baik. Pembangun membina alat yang sebelum ini tidak wujud. Masyarakat sivil menuntut pertanggungjawaban kuasa dengan bukti, bukan sekadar dakwaan.

Harapan jangka panjangnya lebih mudah: agar SPR akhirnya menerbitkan datanya dalam bentuk yang boleh dicapai, dan platform ini menjadi tidak lagi diperlukan.

Sehingga itu, koleksi ini diteruskan. Jika SPR ingin menerima pakai platform ini sebagai miliknya sendiri, tawaran itu kekal tanpa syarat dan tanpa tarikh luput. Kami akan membina dan menyelenggara set data, membangunkan laman ini, dan melakukan apa sahaja yang perlu untuk memastikan latihan serta penerapan institusi yang sewajarnya dalam SPR.

Matlamatnya bukan untuk memiliki ini. Matlamatnya adalah memastikan ia wujud.

</ArticleBody>
Learn how to authenticate your requests to the ElectionData.MY API.
API, authentication, API key, election data

## API Keys

All requests to the ElectionData.MY API must be authenticated using a Bearer token in the `Authorization` header. Requests without a valid API key will receive a `401 Unauthorized` response.

```bash
Authorization: Bearer <your-api-key>
```

We do not allow unauthorised access for one reason only—we built ElectionData.MY to be a serious public good, and therefore need a verified mechanism to contact API users in the event that there are urgent operational issues to resolve (e.g. on election night). This is also why we only ask for an email address (which you can anonymise) and nothing more—we do not need to know who you are or why you want to use the API. If you prefer not to register (and to take responsibility for your own backend), all datasets are available via the [Data Catalogue](/data-catalogue) with options for manual download or unauthorised programmatic access via our open data lake.

## Getting an API Key

API keys are generated through the [API Console](/console). The process takes under a minute:

1. Sign in with your email address—a one-time passcode (OTP) will be sent to your inbox.
2. Once signed in, click **Generate Key**, give it a descriptive name (e.g. _prod-site_ or _local-dev_), and copy it.
3. Include the key as a `Bearer` token in the `Authorization` header of every API request.

<Callout label="Note">You can generate up to **15 API keys** per account—more than enough to maintain separate keys for development, staging, and production.</Callout>

## Sample Request

Here is a complete example showing how to pass your API key:

```bash
curl -X GET "https://api.electiondata.my/v1/candidates?id=CMVBA" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer your-api-key-here"
```

## Guard your Keys

AI is awesome, but it has also made credential leakage dramatically easier—code gets written faster, pasted more freely, and pushed to public repos without a second thought. In this particular instance, there is no particular risk to us from you leaking your keys (because the API is intentionally not rate-limited), but we will hold you accountable for any authenticated requests made using your keys. This means that if your keys are being used to abuse the API, we reserve the right to revoke them and blacklist your IP/email.

Some simple practices you should abide by:
- Do not commit your API key to public repositories. Use environment variables or a secrets manager instead.
- If a key is compromised, revoke it immediately from the API Console and generate a replacement.
- Avoid making API calls directly from client-side code where possible—network requests are visible to anyone with DevTools open. 
- If a backend (even a serverless one) is impractical or unfeasible for you, just be aware that your key will be visible in browser requests and rotate it if you suspect misuse.

## Why no Rate Limit?

Most APIs rate-limit to protect infrastructure costs or enforce tiered pricing. ElectionData.MY has neither concern—the API sits in front of a highly-optimised static data lake, so the marginal cost per query is negligible. Aggressive usage is unlikely to degrade the service for others.

We also think rate limits are the wrong default for a public good. Journalists, researchers, and developers building civic tools should not have to worry about hitting a ceiling on election night. So even if you are querying at scale, if the API suits your workflow, use it freely.
Query election results for any candidate across every contest they have stood in.
HTTP status codes and error response format for the ElectionData.MY API.
API, errors, HTTP status codes, election data

## HTTP Status Codes

The API uses standard HTTP status codes to indicate the success or failure of a request. Codes in the `2xx` range indicate success; codes in the `4xx` range indicate a client error; codes in the `5xx` range indicate a server-side problem.

<StatusTable codes={[
  { code: "200", name: "OK", description: "Request succeeded.", color: "green" },
  { code: "400", name: "Bad Request", description: "Missing or invalid parameters, or missing version prefix.", color: "amber" },
  { code: "401", name: "Unauthorized", description: "Missing or invalid API key.", color: "red" },
  { code: "404", name: "Not Found", description: "Unknown endpoint, or no data found for the given parameters.", color: "amber" },
  { code: "405", name: "Method Not Allowed", description: "Only GET requests are supported.", color: "amber" },
  { code: "500", name: "Internal Server Error", description: "Something went wrong on our end (rare).", color: "red" },
]} />

## Error Response Format

All error responses return the relevant HTTP status code along with a JSON body with a single `error` field containing a human-readable description of what went wrong. For example, a request missing a version prefix will return the following:

```json
HTTP/2 400

{
  "error": "API version required. Use /vN/ prefix, e.g. /v1/candidates"
}
```
Programmatic access to Malaysian election data—candidates, seats, parties, and results.

## What is it?

ElectionData.MY curates and publishes comprehensive data on Malaysian elections from 1954 to the present, covering every single person or party that has ever contested an election in Malaysia. This API is the programmatic interface to that data, specifically for developers who want to query it on demand rather than download static files.

All data is dedicated to the public domain (marked with CC0 1.0 Universal), so you are free to use this API for any purpose, commercial or otherwise, with or without attribution.

The API is free to access. We monitor for abuse, but there is no hard rate limit, and therefore no premium tier. All you need is an API key, which you can generate in seconds from the [API Console](/console).

## Do I need this?

ElectionData.MY offers two methods to programmatically access the data: Direct access to static files via the open data lake (use the links provided via the [Data Catalogue](/data-catalogue)), or this open API.

The API and the data lake serve different use cases. To put it simply, use this API if you want to build public-facing products and don't want to build/host your own backend; use the data lake if you want the entire dataset for analysis, local use, or to spin up your own backend (although you will hopefully first explore this API, which treats developers as first-class citizens).

To put it more thoroughly, use the API (dynamic queries) if…

- You are building a dynamic application that queries election data on demand, e.g. a candidate lookup tool or a live election dashboard.
- You are embedding election data into another product, such as a news app, civic platform, or data pipeline.
- You need a stable, versioned interface with consistent response schemas, rather than writing custom parsing logic against raw files that may change structure over time.

Use the data lake (static files) instead if…

<GrayList items={[
  "You need bulk data for analysis and research.",
  "You are working in Python, R, or a BI tool and prefer direct file downloads over API calls.",
  "You need geospatial or boundary data—we do not expose these via the API, as they are better served as static files (e.g. GeoJSON, GeoParquet, FlatGeobuf).",
  "You want to explore all available datasets interactively before deciding what to build.",
]} />
API versioning policy and stability guarantees for the ElectionData.MY API.
API, versioning, breaking changes, election data

## Current Version

The API is currently at **v1**. All requests must include the version prefix in the URL:

```
https://api.electiondata.my/v1
```

For example: `https://api.electiondata.my/v1/candidates?id=CMVBA`

Requests without a version prefix will receive a `400 Bad Request` response.

## Breaking Changes

All changes within the same version will be backwards compatible, so you can be confident that your products won't break. The following are considered **breaking** changes and will be versioned:

- Removing or renaming existing fields from response objects.
- Changing the type or format of existing response fields.
- Removing or renaming existing endpoints.
- Making previously optional parameters required.

The following are considered **non-breaking** changes and may happen at any time without notice:

<GrayList items={[
  "Adding new optional query parameters to existing endpoints.",
  "Adding new fields to JSON response objects.",
  "Adding new endpoints.",
  "Improving error messages or adding additional detail to existing error responses.",
]} />



## Yes, this isn't REST

Using a `/v1/` prefix is a REST anti-pattern—a true REST API would rely on [HATEOAS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HATEOAS) (Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State) to communicate resource locations and transitions, rather than encoding a version in the path.

This is a deliberate design choice. The primary consumers of this API include journalists, civic society organisations, and developers doing work in support of core public-interest functions. For these users, simplicity and predictability matter more than architectural purity. A URL that says `/v1/candidates` is immediately understandable. HATEOAS is not.
